questions.specification:
What circular strategy is illustrated by the image of a smartphone take-back recycling kiosk?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfGrcxaPZV21-_N3iuE4cM7sunC7xFKGHPSkSW_vICSMeOZHxWfUgQuwx3hC5ZQ-idqBlFTcasewaz3m121Xgr-NUihHQUZRXVtxzxb--alMXPYYA2BJHLTrM35DxSoROh6mJcJ2g?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

The image shows a refillable shampoo station at a supermarket. This demonstrates which design principle?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/7112f31c-ea39-4ab8-81f4-727a960a86c1.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Dematerialisation is the reduction of total material and energy throughput of any product and service. Using a refill station reduces the need for new plastic bottles and encourages reuse.

parts.content: Explain the difference between a linear economy and a circular economy.
parts.markscheme:
- State that a linear economy follows a 'take-make-dispose' model where resources are extracted, used, and then discarded as waste <marks>1 mark</marks>

- State that a circular economy is based on the continuous reuse and recycling of materials to keep resources in use for as long as possible <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Explain that a linear economy often leads to resource depletion and high environmental impact due to its focus on short-term product life <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Explain that a circular economy promotes sustainability and minimal waste by utilizing restorative and regenerative systems <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for each clear, comparative point, up to a maximum of [4].</note>

parts.content: Explain the role of biodegradable materials in a circular economy.
parts.markscheme:
- **Describe** how biodegradable materials break down naturally into non-toxic substances, which reduces the volume of waste sent to landfill <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Explain** how they have a lower environmental impact during disposal as they do not release persistent microplastics or toxins into the environment <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Identify** their role in products with short life cycles, such as food packaging, where they can be disposed of alongside organic waste <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Outline** their compatibility with composting and soil regeneration, which facilitates nutrient cycling within the biological loop of the circular economy <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Describe** how they support zero-waste goals by ensuring materials can safely return to the biosphere through natural processes <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **State** how they can replace non-renewable or petroleum-based inputs with renewable biological sources, reducing dependency on finite resources <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] per well-explained benefit up to a maximum of [6].</note>

questions.specification: A company that builds products to last, repair, and repurpose is promoting:
A product that can be disassembled and repaired aligns with which circular economy principle?

options.markscheme: This principle focuses on the initial design phase to prevent waste and pollution from being created in the first place, rather than the maintenance of products already in circulation.

options.content: Keeping products and materials in use
options.markscheme: This principle (part of the technical cycle) focuses on extending the life of products and materials through strategies such as maintenance, repair, reuse, refurbishment, and remanufacturing.
This principle applies to the biological cycle and involves restoring and enhancing nature, rather than the repair of technical or manufactured goods.
This is a linear economy strategy where products are designed to have a limited life, which is the opposite of circular economy principles.

parts.content: Compare the effectiveness of recycling vs. reuse strategies in circular product systems.
parts.markscheme:
- Recycling involves reprocessing waste materials into new products, whereas reuse involves using an object again in its original form <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reuse is generally more energy-efficient than recycling because it avoids the energy-intensive reprocessing and manufacturing stages <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recycling allows for the reintroduction of materials into the production cycle even if the original product has degraded or is no longer functional <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reuse is more effective for extending the life of durable, high-value products designed for longevity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recycling is often better suited for short-lived, low-complexity, or disposable products where reuse is not feasible <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The effectiveness of both strategies depends on product design (e.g., modularity for reuse vs. material purity for recycling) and available infrastructure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Economic trade-offs exist between the costs of collection and sorting for recycling versus the costs of cleaning and reverse logistics for reuse <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Both strategies involve environmental trade-offs, such as emissions from industrial reprocessing vs. the environmental impact of transport for reuse systems <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A final judgement comparing their relative contributions to closing resource loops within a circular economy <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award [1] per clearly explained and comparative point, up to [9 marks max].

questions.specification: How do biodegradable materials support the principles of a circular economy?

options.content: By persisting in the environment indefinitely
By safely breaking down into natural components
options.markscheme: In a circular economy, biodegradable materials are part of the biological cycle. They support circularity by safely decomposing into natural, non-toxic components that return nutrients to the biosphere.

questions.specification:
What concept is reflected in this image of discarded fast fashion waste in a landfill?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXc3rguvnqlMHOIMxLFLbYOtIqiw3qP_s9FUSZD0Ypvh9nAW4Q3E8e3m85YcbJs2dT6r2fX_khuM8MylYdXGWjrNxDsZ1XdhYwoUQlzmXz8c63zxTLPB76Ql1xM_4uB6lTLMeDmaKg?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)
Which practice supports recovery in circular systems?
Which strategy is central to achieving circular design?

The image shows single-use plates manufactured from banana leaves. Which sustainable material strategy is best represented by this product?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/ea6bacbd-900b-4c51-85b0-997df0c112bb.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Upgradability refers to the design strategy of allowing a product to be improved over time with newer components (e.g., software or hardware), which is not applicable to a single-use organic product.

Design for disassembly is a strategy that ensures a product can be easily taken apart at the end of its life to facilitate recycling or reuse of components. These plates are made from a single material and do not require disassembly.
This refers to the industrial process of using technology (like sensors or magnets) to sort mixed waste in recycling facilities, rather than the selection of the material itself.

Banana leaves are natural organic materials that decompose through the action of microorganisms, making them a sustainable choice for single-use products that would otherwise contribute to landfill waste.

questions.specification: What is an expected benefit of a circular economy?

options.content: Lower raw material costs over time
Increased product lifespan through repair and reuse
More single-use products to boost convenience

questions.specification: What best describes the circular economy’s approach to product end-of-life?
Which process aligns **least** with the principles of a circular economy?

options.markscheme: Energy recovery (such as incineration) is considered the least circular option as it involves the destruction of materials to capture energy, effectively removing them from the circular system. In contrast, reuse, biodegradable design (biological cycle), and closed-loop production aim to maintain materials within the economy.

parts.content: Discuss how take-back legislation supports the recovery and reuse of materials.
parts.markscheme:
- Encourages producers to be responsible for end-of-life products <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides infrastructure for collection and sorting <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Incentivises repair, remanufacturing or recycling <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces illegal dumping and waste <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fosters innovation in product design to meet recovery targets <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps achieve closed-loop systems <marks>1 mark</marks>

6 marks total

<note>Award [1] per valid, clearly explained point (up to 6 max).</note>

questions.specification: A circular design consultancy is showcasing global product innovations that illustrate how materials and systems can be looped or recovered. The three figures below show examples from different industries.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a bicycle designed with a single aluminium alloy frame that is fully recyclable and powder-coated for durability.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/1QzYB_XacMpZnCseOJ0_v)

i.) Identify the type of resource recovery strategy used in this bicycle design.
parts.markscheme:
- Material recycling. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 max] for correctly identifying recycling as the strategy involved in reclaiming the aluminium material.*

parts.content: ii.) Explain two reasons why aluminium is a material well-suited to circular economy models.
parts.markscheme:
- Can be recycled repeatedly without degrading material quality. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- High scrap value encourages recovery and reprocessing. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [2 max] for explanations tied to material longevity, recyclability, and economic viability.*

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a refill station at a supermarket where customers fill reusable containers with liquid soap, detergent, or shampoo.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/jLeLa-6HXV8g6rpZU6jQP)

i.) Outline three circular benefits of refill systems like the one shown.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduces the need for single-use plastic bottles. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Encourages consumer participation in product life extension. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Cuts down manufacturing and transportation waste. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [3 max] for circular design benefits that reduce waste and close the consumption loop.*

parts.content: ii.) Analyse how such refill systems might face limitations when scaled globally.
parts.markscheme:
- Varying consumer habits may reduce adoption in certain cultures. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Logistics of maintaining clean, well-stocked stations can be complex. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Requires investment in infrastructure and consumer education. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- May not be viable for all product types due to hygiene concerns. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [4 max] for a range of realistic challenges to global implementation of reuse models.*

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a modular office furniture system where worn parts (legs, arms, upholstery) can be individually replaced and returned to the manufacturer for material recovery.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/MVwpYN7rPrgFIBdCJozI-)

i.) Explain how modularity supports the principles of both longevity and resource cycling in the circular economy.
parts.markscheme:
- Damaged parts can be swapped without discarding the whole product, extending lifespan. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Components can be disassembled easily for material separation and recycling. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Enables standardized repair processes that reduce waste. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [3 max] for clear connection to circular economy values such as durability, modularity, and recoverability.*

parts.content: ii.) Evaluate how businesses can benefit from implementing such circular modular systems.
parts.markscheme:
- Builds customer loyalty through repair services or part upgrades. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Lowers long-term costs by reducing need for full product replacements. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Creates opportunities for take-back and resale programs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Enhances sustainability branding, attracting eco-conscious clients. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Requires upfront R&D to design for disassembly and compatibility. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- May involve higher short-term costs in logistics and reverse supply chains. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [6 max] for a balanced evaluation of long-term business and operational advantages and drawbacks.*

questions.specification: An international design award is celebrating companies that have embraced circular economy principles to reduce environmental impact. Three nominated products are described below, each representing a different design strategy.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows refurbished office chairs sold through a furniture take-back scheme. The chair uses recovered components and is sold at a lower price than new models.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/jMuhzFMMkWY6gV--lSdJ5)

State one way this design contributes to a circular economy.
parts.markscheme:
• It supports reuse through product refurbishment. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 max] for identifying reuse, recovery, or extended life as a circular economy strategy.*

parts.content: Explain two environmental benefits of product take-back schemes.
parts.markscheme:
• Reduce landfill waste by collecting used products. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Save energy and resources by reusing parts instead of manufacturing new ones. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [2 max] for clearly explained environmental advantages of take-back programs.*

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a set of flat-pack wooden shelves made entirely from FSC-certified wood, designed to be disassembled and repaired easily with basic tools.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/G-v-ygbEu6r_4ku0Dpm-j)

Identify three design features that extend the shelf’s life cycle.
parts.markscheme:
• Use of durable wood materials. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Modular design allows for disassembly and repair. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Standard fittings make parts easy to replace. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [3 max] for features that promote repair, reuse, or replacement of components.*

parts.content: Discuss how disassembly and modularity support circular economy objectives.
parts.markscheme:
• Make it easier to repair individual parts. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Facilitate component reuse in future products. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Simplify recycling at end-of-life by separating materials. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Extend product life by allowing upgrades or reconfiguration. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [4 max] for valid points explaining how disassembly and modular design aid waste reduction and resource reuse.*

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a solar-powered garden light with a rechargeable battery, designed to operate off-grid using renewable energy.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/xUeV3SIhaT7gw88khuk7y)

Describe one advantage of using solar energy in this product.
parts.markscheme:
• Reduces dependence on non-renewable electricity sources. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 max] for a valid advantage related to sustainability or circular energy use.*

parts.content: Evaluate the role of renewable energy in supporting circular economy design.
parts.markscheme:
• Powers products without depleting fossil resources. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Reduces carbon footprint over the product's lifetime. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Supports decentralised energy use in off-grid applications. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Aligns with circular goals of long-term environmental sustainability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Complements other design strategies like energy-efficient components. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Enables energy independence for low-power consumer goods. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [6 max] for detailed evaluation of how renewable energy contributes to circular design principles.*

questions.specification: A key goal of the circular economy is to:

options.content: Maximise the use of virgin raw materials
Prioritise disposal over repair and reuse

questions.specification:
What does a wind farm powering an electric vehicle charging station represent in the circular economy?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/4f4248ca-bcbe-40c9-9ef2-f68eb70f4b0f.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Transitioning to renewable energy sources is a fundamental principle of the circular economy, ensuring that the energy used to power technical and biological cycles is sustainable and non-depleting.

questions.specification: Why are biodegradable materials important in circular design?
A takeaway food company switches to compostable containers. This change reflects:

A sustainability consultancy is working with designers to evaluate how well their product ranges meet the aims of a circular economy. They’ve collected several example case studies to support internal training on product longevity, biodegradability, and renewable energy use.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a modular sofa design where each cushion, frame part, and fabric cover can be individually replaced or upgraded. The design avoids glue and instead uses clips and zippers to allow for easy disassembly.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2SOaoUbyfDG9sWMPFo6VU)

State one way this design approach contributes to sustainability.
parts.markscheme: - Extends the product’s usable life by enabling partial replacements. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using examples, explain two reasons why designing for disassembly benefits manufacturers and consumers.
parts.markscheme:
- Manufacturers can refurbish components more easily, reducing material costs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Consumers are more likely to repair rather than discard products. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a stack of takeaway containers made from sugarcane pulp (bagasse). These are marketed as biodegradable and compostable alternatives to polystyrene foam.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/v59f83G407xLUjas7iwDF)

Describe three reasons why biodegradable materials like bagasse are appropriate for food packaging.
parts.markscheme:
- Break down in composting conditions without releasing harmful substances. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Often made from agricultural waste, reducing the need for virgin materials. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Appeal to environmentally conscious consumers and meet regulatory trends. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline four challenges designers face when using biodegradable materials.
parts.markscheme:
- Shorter shelf-life or durability compared to traditional plastics. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limited resistance to moisture, heat, or oil. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Restricted access to industrial composting facilities in some regions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Misleading labeling can confuse consumers about proper disposal. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a house with solar panels installed on the rooftops. The panels are used to power household appliances and to charge electric vehicles, reducing grid dependency.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/qA00u5_-8a6Bh1drXTqYD)

Identify one renewable energy source shown in this figure.
parts.markscheme: - Solar energy. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how renewable energy supports the goals of the circular economy.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduces reliance on fossil fuels and lowers greenhouse gas emissions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Powers manufacturing and recycling processes sustainably. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enables energy independence in product life cycles. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Complements low-impact design approaches like distributed production. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports carbon-neutral branding and sustainability certifications. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages integration of clean technologies across the product lifecycle. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: In a circular economy, materials are typically:
Which of the following would be **LEAST** aligned with circular economy values?

parts.content: Outline four strategies that designers can use to eliminate waste in product development.
parts.markscheme:
- Design for disassembly to allow reuse of parts <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Use of biodegradable materials <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Dematerialisation – using fewer materials <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Modular design for upgradability and repair <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] per clearly explained strategy (up to 4 max).</note>

questions.specification:
What principle of the circular economy is best illustrated by this system of reusable glass milk bottles?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/34f70a41-69e4-4a50-8909-e47dc91679be.jpeg)

options.markscheme: While the circular economy aims to be powered by renewable energy, the reuse of glass bottles specifically illustrates the material circulation principle.
The system shown is circular (reuse), not linear (take-make-dispose).

options.content: Keep products and materials in use
options.markscheme: The reuse of glass milk bottles is a classic example of this principle, where products are kept at their highest utility and value through multiple cycles of cleaning and refilling.
Product obsolescence (designing products to fail or become outdated) is a feature of linear economies, not circular ones.

parts.content: Identify two key characteristics of a circular economy.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify that resources are repurposed or reused in a closed-loop system <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Identify that waste and pollution are minimised at every stage of the product lifecycle <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award [1] for each accurate feature identified.</note>

questions.specification:
Which circular design principle is most evident in the image of a laptop being upgraded by swapping out modular parts?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXeuaWNMkeuc9VhWLnbYTZrhSBmQrY8p70ITTF10rZhO7KUPpR8vu6M7c4wSqMYwbDnksmzJ1jHY7cTPwZBHt8m6PuY4Px_jXwFFweLf3yf7_QmhmfcoU6Rt_3KPCvAqoCqiOiNN?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)
What does renewable energy offer to the circular economy?

parts.content: Describe how renewable energy contributes to a circular economy.
parts.markscheme:
- **Reduces** dependence on finite fossil fuels by substituting depletable resources with regenerative energy flows <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Decreases** greenhouse gas emissions, which helps minimize the environmental impact of material processing and logistics <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Powers** the energy-intensive technical cycles, such as closed-loop manufacturing and recycling processes <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Supports** the regeneration of natural systems by avoiding the ecological degradation associated with resource extraction <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for each valid explanation up to a maximum of [4].</note>

questions.specification:
What circular economy objective is demonstrated by compostable packaging?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/516d5de6-9940-4513-96cf-95882d4021a6.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Repair is an objective within the technical cycle of the circular economy, focusing on extending the life of products made from finite materials.

Compostable packaging is designed to be broken down by biological processes and returned to the biosphere as nutrients. This demonstrates the circular economy objective of using biodegradable materials to regenerate natural systems.
While reducing energy consumption is a broad goal of sustainability, it is not the specific objective demonstrated by the use of compostable materials.
This relates to supply chain management and efficiency rather than the cycle of material recovery and regeneration.

questions.specification:
Which key circular economy benefit is demonstrated by the practice of repairing sneakers shown in the image?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXcERHJqUpg8HnYi9Eix6-DTX9R9feJHRDN4NcAywa4wiB4U_3FYihdxcqnrh7RakclZiQ8A_VpvMYlpESudZy8A33w4biuEg0LvagV4Ae8z-6oDKwzJ4RUmclaRQ_kO2k_WEi0Bgg?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.markscheme: Repairing products like sneakers is a fundamental practice in a circular economy. It follows the principle of keeping products and materials in use for as long as possible, thereby restoring their utility and recovering their value without the need for new raw materials.

questions.specification: Which feature distinguishes a circular economy from a linear economy?

parts.content: Evaluate how circular design principles can be applied across a product’s life cycle.
parts.markscheme:
- Select durable or biodegradable materials at the design stage to reduce resource depletion <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use renewable energy and minimize waste during production to lower the carbon footprint <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Utilize reusable or minimal packaging during distribution to reduce solid waste <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourage longevity and repairability during the use phase to extend product life <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enable disassembly for reuse or recycling at the end-of-life stage to close the material loop <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain how circular design supports sustainable resource use and reduces the overall environmental footprint <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify higher upfront costs for materials and infrastructure as a limitation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Discuss consumer habits and infrastructure limitations as significant challenges <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Conclude on the long-term effectiveness and value of circular design for global sustainability <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] per well-justified point, up to [9 max].</note>

questions.specification:
What circular principle is demonstrated by a solar-powered manufacturing facility?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfeTOXW0naP9krUf662lW5UE_csZ1YYCLWkYidISkU9hSm0566KATAz2YN_bt7jYPigy7Jnshs-_0w_cgVfQat9hmWtnSDTLYtOOHcdPvjP0t5Epw-OjF1LkIGEvH2ht4E1fRISag?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)
Which of these materials is most consistent with circular principles?

A product development team is preparing a sustainability audit of their latest consumer goods portfolio. They’ve selected several design examples that demonstrate different circular economy principles, including closed-loop systems, product longevity, biodegradability, and take-back schemes.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a closed-loop aluminium can recycling system, where used cans are collected, melted, and reformed into new cans indefinitely with minimal material loss.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/hKbT9DJzeZTCBzO1vswBV)

State the term used to describe this type of circular process.
parts.markscheme: - Closed-loop system. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain two advantages of closed-loop recycling systems for manufacturers.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduces raw material extraction costs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enhances brand reputation for sustainability, attracting eco-conscious consumers. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a pair of running shoes that use modular components and bio-based materials. The shoes are designed to be disassembled by the user for repair or recycling.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/rmkAcI2V237HpJ20sBQx3)

Describe three design strategies shown in this product that support the circular economy.
parts.markscheme:
- Modular components enable partial repair or upgrade. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use of biodegradable materials reduces landfill impact. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disassembly allows for component-level recycling. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline four ways this design might reduce environmental impact across the product’s life cycle.
parts.markscheme:
- Less frequent replacement due to repairability. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Decreased waste at end-of-life due to biodegradability. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lower emissions from bio-based material production. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced energy use during recycling thanks to simplified disassembly. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a clothing brand’s in-store garment take-back scheme. Customers return old clothes for store credit; items are sorted for reuse, repair, or recycling.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ynKRhHZdX2ghVo9A3EBAx)

Identify one strategy visible in this scheme that aligns with circular economy objectives.
parts.markscheme: - Take-back or product return initiative. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how take-back systems contribute to minimizing product waste and pollution.
parts.markscheme:
- Enable companies to capture materials for reuse or recycling. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Prevent textiles from entering landfill or incinerators. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourage consumers to adopt more responsible disposal habits. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Create closed-loop systems within the fashion industry. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Support refurbishment or donation of wearable items. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Help track material flows and inform sustainable product redesign. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which energy source would be most problematic for a circular economy?
What does a circular economy aim to achieve?

options.content: Eliminate waste throughout the product life cycle
Maximize profit at the cost of sustainability

questions.specification: Which energy source best supports circular economy principles?

options.markscheme: Coal is a finite fossil fuel that contributes to a linear 'take-make-dispose' model and high carbon emissions.
Diesel is a finite petroleum product and its use is inconsistent with the transition to renewable energy required for a circular economy.
Solar energy is a renewable resource. The circular economy model emphasizes a transition to renewable energy sources to regenerate natural systems and reduce reliance on finite resources.
Natural gas is a finite fossil fuel. While it may have lower emissions than coal, it still relies on the extraction of non-renewable resources.

questions.specification: A manufacturing firm is transitioning from a traditional linear model to a circular business framework. They are re-evaluating their material flows and product relationships to ensure that resources remain in productive use for as long as possible, using the Cradle to Cradle framework to categorize their outputs.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** displays the ‘Butterfly Diagram’ used in circular economy planning, illustrating the flow of materials through two distinct cycles: biological and technical.

![Circular economy butterfly diagram showing biological and technical loops](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-f5f6ae9e-5c18-4212-80a6-a1e301ba28cb-1771938518510/3add51f1-8954-4ffb-b964-580e54988adf.jpeg)

State one defining characteristic of a 'technical nutrient' within the technical cycle.
parts.markscheme:
• It is a non-biological material (e.g., metals, plastics) that can be continuously recovered and reused without losing its quality or entering the biosphere. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 max] for identifying a core property such as being non-biodegradable or its capability for infinite recovery loops.*

parts.content: Explain why the strict separation of biological and technical cycles is a fundamental requirement for a successful circular economy.
parts.markscheme:
• Prevents contamination: Mixing technical materials (like heavy metals) into biological cycles can poison the biosphere/compost. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Maintains material purity: Biological additives in technical materials (like organic fibers in plastic) can make high-grade recycling or remanufacture impossible. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [2 max] for explanations focusing on environmental safety and material recovery efficiency.*

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a smart washing machine designed for a 'Product-as-a-Service' (PaaS) model. Instead of buying the machine, customers pay a small fee per wash cycle, and the manufacturer retains ownership and responsibility for the appliance.

![Smart washing machine with a digital display showing pay-per-use subscription status](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-f5f6ae9e-5c18-4212-80a6-a1e301ba28cb-1771938518510/38b75dec-1840-47ac-8249-5202e29e1ee3.jpeg)

Describe three advantages of the 'Product-as-a-Service' model for the consumer or the environment.
parts.markscheme:
• Lowers the financial barrier for consumers to access high-quality, energy-efficient appliances. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Shifts the incentive to the manufacturer to produce durable, long-lasting products rather than planned obsolescence. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Ensures professional maintenance and end-of-life recovery, reducing illegal dumping or improper recycling. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [3 max] for distinct advantages covering financial, quality, or environmental themes.*

parts.content: Outline four design strategies used to improve the 'emotional durability' and 'physical reliability' of high-end appliances to extend their lifespan.
parts.markscheme:
• Use of high-quality, wear-resistant materials (e.g., stainless steel instead of plastic) for physical reliability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Classic or 'timeless' aesthetic styling to prevent the product from looking outdated quickly (emotional durability). <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Design for upgradability (e.g., software updates or modular hardware) to keep the product functionally relevant. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Creating a 'story' or user-connection through personalization options that discourage the user from wanting a new model. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [4 max] for strategies that address both the physical breakdown and the psychological desire to replace the product.*

parts.content:
**Figure 3** compares two assembly methods for a product housing: a reversible snap-fit joint (View A) and a permanent heat-staked fusion (View B).

![Technical diagram comparing reversible snap-fit joints and permanent heat-staked joints](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-f5f6ae9e-5c18-4212-80a6-a1e301ba28cb-1771938518510/7c1a7ade-bf4d-4196-ba31-da7f545c99a2.jpeg)

State how the use of the reversible joint in View A facilitates the 'remanufacturing' of a product.
parts.markscheme:
• It allows for non-destructive access to internal components, ensuring the outer casing can be reused without damage during the rebuilding process. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 max] for identifying that it avoids damage to the parts during opening.*

parts.content: Discuss why 'remanufacturing' is considered a more effective circular economy strategy than 'recycling' for complex technical products.
parts.markscheme:
• Energy preservation: Remanufacturing retains the 'embodied energy' of the original manufacturing process, whereas recycling requires energy to melt or shred materials. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Value retention: Remanufacturing keeps the high-value shape and function of the part, whereas recycling reduces it to raw material of lower financial value. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Material integrity: Recycling often results in 'downcycling' where material quality degrades; remanufacturing maintains the original material specifications. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Reduced emissions: The carbon footprint of cleaning and replacing a single gear is significantly lower than manufacturing a whole new gear from recycled pellets. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Resource efficiency: Remanufacturing uses fewer new resources to achieve the same product utility. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Economic viability: It creates higher-skilled labor opportunities for inspection and re-assembly compared to bulk material processing. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [6 max] for a comprehensive discussion comparing energy, value, quality, and environmental impact.*

parts.content: State two ways designers can extend the longevity of a product.
parts.markscheme:
- Designing for durability using high-quality materials <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allowing for easy maintenance and repair <marks>1 mark</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each relevant method.</note>

questions.specification: A circular economy encourages manufacturers to:

topic.title: C2.2 Design for a circular economy
